scenario: Helix Fintech transforms FEEL output across 6000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A DMN rule earlier in the flow produced a list of contexts of the form [{key:"a", value:1}, {key:"b", value:2}] via get entries(); a script task must assemble that list back into a single context for the next decision input.
question: Which FEEL function constructs a context from a list of key-value pair contexts and is the reverse of get entries()?

options.text: Call context(entries) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context
Call context put(context, key, value) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context
Call get value(context, keys) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context
Call context merge(contexts) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context

a.text: Correct. context(entries) accepts a list<context> where each element has a string key entry and a value entry; it returns the assembled context and is documented as the reverse of get entries().

b.text: Incorrect. context put adds a single key and value to one existing context and returns the updated context; it does not interpret a list of {key, value} contexts and is not the reverse of get entries().

c.text: Incorrect. get value reads a value from a single context at a nested path defined by keys; it does not assemble a context from a list of key-value pair contexts.

d.text: Incorrect. context merge unions a list of whole contexts; it treats each list element as a full context to merge, not as a single {key, value} pair to assemble into one entry.

explanation: context(entries) constructs a context from a list of contexts that each carry a string key entry and a value entry; it is the reverse of get entries() and returns null if any element is not a context or lacks the required entries.
